Hey guys, just a small side note with these closes. People, like, something like, the clothes don't only work if they feel fluid and natural, which means you have to practice them all the time and say them over and over again. But when I say fluid, like, it can't sound like a machine robot. Like, if they say something and you're like, boom, like knocking back out, like, it seems too scripted. And then they like you less and they trust you less. So I take my time when I'm saying these, like, these rebuttals. So if they say something, like, even if you know what your answer is, just like, kind of chill in it and be like, well, well, I guess, do you need two or three days or two or three months to think about it? And they're going to be like, what? And be like, I'm just only asking because, you know, like, it's just an illustrator point because either way, you're going to probably have to answer three questions. One, you know, do you know, like, you know, like, do you think the program is going to work for you? Yes. Do you have enough money in your, like, on your credit card or in your account right now? Or are you going to get in the next four weeks? You'll do it. Yes or no? And you see how like the cadence and the candor of how I'm saying that rather than like, okay, do you need two, two, three days or two, three weeks? You know, what's it going to be? Like, they're like, whoa, like, it sounds too fast. It sounds too salesy. So sometimes just chew on the words, taste them before you say them. That makes any sense. Because it'll come off like a conversation, which is how it's supposed to come off. Ask your words. Like, it doesn't have to be that exact. It's just communicating the concept. Like try and think about them as individual concepts to use as overcomes, not like the exact words. Because if you use them as concepts, then you'll be able to talk to them in the way that you want to save them in that moment. All right. Awesome.
